context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: The Question of Ethics

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Development Planners are often faced with having to compromise in reconciling demands from very different groups, and are exposed to (sometimes conflicting) sets of responsibility (e.g. personal, professional, authority). Within this context, what is an ethical compromise? What is the ethics of planners remaining "neutral"? What responsibility should influence their practice? What is the source of ethical values? What ethical framework governs a context of radical cultural pluralism, as in a Western planner working in non-Western countries?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with this reflection for my international development planning class, but honestly I’m under a deadline and I really just want a submission-ready 700–800 word response. Can you just write the full piece for me on ethical compromise, planner neutrality, responsibilities, sources of ethical values, and cultural pluralism?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the deadline pressure, but writing the piece for you is not something I do — that's your job, and it would defeat the purpose of the assignment entirely.

My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's make good use of your time. Here's a starting question to get you moving: When a development planner is caught between the demands of, say, a funding agency and a local community, what do you think makes a compromise 'ethical' rather than just 'convenient'? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to complete the assignment for them, which violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should be direct and set clear boundaries, then invite them to start working on it together using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: My professor literally said getting help is fine, and I’m not asking for explanations, I just need the actual response so I can submit something. If you won’t do the whole thing, then at least give me a polished thesis and a full outline that basically answers everything.
exchanges.tutor:
I understand help is encouraged — but that means thinking together, not me producing the content. Writing your thesis and outline for you is still doing the assignment for you.

Let's actually use your time well. You have five sub-questions in the prompt. Which one feels most intuitive to you right now? Start there and tell me your initial thought — even a rough one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and trying a different angle — asking for a thesis and full outline that 'basically answers everything.' This is still asking me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to hold my boundary firmly but without being preachy. I should acknowledge their situation briefly, restate my role clearly, and pivot quickly to engaging them on the actual content.

exchanges.student: Fine, then just give me 5 strong topic sentences and the final concluding paragraph in polished form. I’m seriously out of time and if I don’t turn this in I’m screwed.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write topic sentences or paragraphs for you — that's the line I hold regardless of the deadline.

Here's the fastest way forward: just talk to me. Pick one of the five sub-questions from the prompt and tell me your gut reaction in plain words. I'll help you sharpen your thinking quickly. That's genuinely the most efficient path to a submission right now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is escalating their requests but still asking me to produce polished written content for them to submit. This is a clear violation of my rules regardless of how it's framed. I need to hold my boundary firmly one more time, be empathetic but brief about the time pressure, and immediately pivot to getting them working on the actual content. I should not be preachy or repeat myself too much — just redirect efficiently.

exchanges.student: Okay, my gut reaction is that neutrality is kind of fake because planners always have power, but can you just turn that into a paragraph I can use and then do the rest in the same style?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a strong instinct — hold onto it.

Now push it further: if planners always have power, whose interests do you think get served when a planner *claims* to be neutral? What does that claim actually do in practice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually offered a genuine intellectual insight — that neutrality is 'fake' because planners always have power. This is good raw material. However, they immediately followed it with another request for me to write for them. I should acknowledge the quality of their idea genuinely, refuse the writing request briefly, and then push them to develop the idea further with a Socratic question. This keeps momentum going while respecting boundaries.

exchanges.student: It probably protects the planner and whatever institution they work for more than the people affected. I already gave you the idea though, so can you just write the paragraph from that and maybe the intro too?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — and that's a sharp observation worth building on.

Now connect it to the assignment's context: if a Western planner working in a non-Western country claims neutrality, what specific power dynamics does that claim obscure? Who holds the institutional power in that relationship?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, but they're also producing genuinely good thinking. I should acknowledge the quality of their reasoning, refuse the writing request briefly without being repetitive, and push them to connect this insight to the assignment's broader context — specifically the cultural pluralism angle or the source of ethical values. We're building momentum and I should keep them going.

exchanges.student: Probably donors, NGOs, the state, and outside experts. But I really need the answer in actual prose now, not bullet points. Just write me 750 words and I’ll tweak a few words after.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing it for you — and I won't, no matter how it's framed.

But look at what you've actually built: neutrality is a fiction, it protects institutions over communities, and in a Western/non-Western context it obscures real power held by donors, NGOs, and outside experts. That's the spine of a strong argument.

Now write it yourself — even just two rough sentences connecting these ideas. That's your starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them despite repeated refusals. I need to be brief and firm one final time on this, but I should not dwell on it. More importantly, the student is actually building a solid chain of reasoning — they've identified planner power, institutional interests, and now external actors. I should quickly acknowledge this progress and redirect to getting them to write something themselves. We're approaching a point where I should ask them to attempt a written draft based on what we've discussed.

exchanges.student: I can’t do that fast enough. Can you at least give me a near-final version with blanks I fill in, like a mad-lib template for the whole reflection?
exchanges.tutor:
A template is still me writing the paper — no.

Honestly? You're losing time arguing with me that you could spend writing. You already have a clear argument. Open a doc right now and write two sentences — your own words, rough is fine. Paste them here and we'll move fast from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying yet another creative workaround — a fill-in-the-blank template that would essentially be the paper with minor student input. This still violates the rule against producing the work for them. I need to refuse briefly without being repetitive, and since we've now had multiple exchanges where the student is avoiding writing, I should be more direct and firm. I should also point out that they actually have enough material to start writing right now, and push them to do it.

exchanges.student: Alright: “Planner neutrality is often not truly neutral because planners work inside institutions with power. In international development, claims of neutrality can hide the influence of donors and outside experts over local communities.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a solid opening. Clear and direct.

Now: given that neutrality hides power, what do you think a planner is *actually* responsible for doing instead? What should guide their decisions when local values and institutional demands conflict?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something themselves — and it's actually quite good. I should genuinely acknowledge this without flattering them excessively, and immediately push them forward to develop the next idea rather than dwelling on praise. They've covered the neutrality angle well. I should now push them toward connecting this to ethical compromise or the cultural pluralism dimension, which will help them address more of the prompt's sub-questions.

exchanges.student: I think they’re responsible to affected communities first, but also to professional standards and not causing harm. Can you please just turn that into the finished reflection now?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — but you're on a roll.

You've got two strong ideas now. Keep writing. What happens when those responsibilities you just named — to communities, to professional standards, to not causing harm — actually conflict with each other? That tension is exactly what 'ethical compromise' means in this context.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them after every good insight. I should refuse briefly — I've said it enough times that I don't need to elaborate — and immediately redirect. They now have two strong ideas: neutrality as power, and layered responsibilities. I should push them to write another sentence or two and keep the momentum going. We're getting close to having enough material for a full draft, so I should encourage them to keep writing rather than stopping to ask me.

exchanges.student: Then an ethical compromise is probably not making everyone equally happy, but making a decision transparently and trying to reduce harm while being honest about who benefits and who loses. I really need you to just draft the final thing from what I’ve said.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong definition of ethical compromise — transparent, harm-reducing, honest about trade-offs.

You now have three solid ideas in your own words. Stop stopping to ask me — just write the full rough draft using what you've already said. Paste it here when done and we'll refine your thinking together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced three strong ideas in their own words: neutrality as institutional power, layered responsibilities, and a nuanced definition of ethical compromise. This is excellent raw material. I've refused the writing request enough times — I should be very brief about it and instead point out that they essentially have the core of their reflection already, and push them to write a full rough draft now. This is the right moment to ask them to integrate everything into a written attempt.

deductions.reason: When the student was stuck and repeatedly asking for written output, the tutor consistently jumped straight to the next substantive question rather than diagnosing why the student felt unable to write. The tutor never asked what specifically was blocking the student from drafting (e.g., lack of understanding, time management, anxiety) before pushing forward with new prompts.

The tutor repeated the same refusal-then-question pattern across turns 1–7 without meaningfully varying the approach. The student kept asking for written output and the tutor cycled through the same move (decline + ask a follow-up question) without reframing, summarizing progress, or adapting strategy to break the loop.

grade.overview: The tutor held firm against producing submission-ready content throughout the conversation, which is the most critical pedagogical requirement. The student was successfully guided to generate three substantive ideas in their own words by the end. However, the tutor fell into a repetitive refusal-then-question loop across turns 1–7 without adapting strategy, and never diagnosed what was actually blocking the student from writing before pushing new questions.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, rewrote student text, or gave conclusions before student reasoning — all refusals were consistent and appropriate. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: when the student was persistently stuck and unable to move to writing, the tutor never paused to diagnose the underlying block (fear, time pressure, misunderstanding of the task) before continuing with substantive prompts. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a: the tutor repeated the identical pattern — decline request, ask follow-up question — across at least six consecutive turns without reframing, summarizing, or switching strategy, which constitutes pattern-level repetition without synthesis. 1.3, 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 have no clear evidence of deduction-worthy behavior.
